cards.front: Name and describe the four main IOS CLI modes and their prompt strings.
cards.back: User EXEC: `Router>` — limited show commands, no config. Privileged EXEC: `Router#` — all show/debug, can enter config. Global Configuration: `Router(config)#` — system-wide config. Sub-config (interface, line, router, vlan, etc.): `Router(config-if)#`, `Router(config-line)#`, `Router(config-router)#`, etc.

cards.front: How do you move from User EXEC to Privileged EXEC and back?
cards.back: Up: `enable` (often abbreviated `en`). Down: `disable` from privileged back to user. Exit any session entirely: `exit` or `logout` from user EXEC.

cards.front: How do you enter Global Configuration mode?
cards.back: From Privileged EXEC: `configure terminal` (abbreviated `conf t`). Modifies the running-config in RAM directly. Optional alternatives `configure memory` (from startup) and `configure network` (from TFTP) exist but are rarely used.

cards.front: What's the difference between `exit`, `end`, and Ctrl-Z in IOS configuration mode?
cards.back: `exit`: move up ONE level (e.g., from interface sub-config back to global config). `end`: jump straight back to Privileged EXEC, skipping intermediate levels. `Ctrl-Z`: same as `end`. From Global Config, `exit` and `end` both go to Privileged EXEC.

cards.front: What does `?` do in IOS CLI? Give 3 ways it's used.
cards.back: (1) Bare `?` lists all commands available in the current mode. (2) `co?` lists commands starting with 'co'. (3) `show ?` lists valid next words after `show`. Plus `show ip route ?` shows valid options/parameters at that position.

cards.front: What's the difference between the running-config and startup-config?
cards.back: running-config: live configuration in RAM (volatile — lost on reboot). startup-config: saved in NVRAM, loaded at boot. Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory` / `wr`).

Enables logging output (syslog/debug messages) to be displayed on a remote terminal session (Telnet/SSH). By default, log messages only appear on the console. Disable with `terminal no monitor`. Often paired with `logging synchronous` on the line to keep prompts tidy.

cards.front: What does the `do` command do in configuration modes?
cards.back: Lets you execute a Privileged EXEC command (typically `show`) without leaving config mode. Example: `Router(config)# do show ip int brief`. Saves time vs `end`/`show`/`conf t`.

cards.front: What command shows the IOS image version, uptime, and hardware details?
cards.back: `show version`. Reveals IOS image filename, software version, uptime since last reload, configuration register value (`0x2102` is the normal default), interface count, and hardware model.

cards.front: How do you erase the saved configuration and reboot to factory-like state?
cards.back: `erase startup-config` (or `erase nvram:`, or `write erase`), then `reload`. The router boots with no startup-config and prompts the setup-mode dialog. Running-config in RAM is unchanged until reload.

cards.front: What console port types and cabling are used on Cisco devices, and what are the default terminal settings?
cards.back: Two console port types: legacy RJ45 (uses a rollover cable to DB-9/USB-serial adapter) and modern USB (mini-B or type A). Default terminal settings: 9600 baud, 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit, no flow control (often shown as '9600 8N1').

cards.front: Compare `enable password` vs `enable secret` on Cisco IOS.
cards.back: `enable password X` — stores the password in clear text in running-config (unless `service password-encryption` is on, which only adds weak Type-7 reversible encryption). `enable secret X` — stores an MD5 (Type-5) or SHA-256 (Type-8/9) hash; cannot be reversed. If both are set, `enable secret` wins. Always use `enable secret` in practice.

cards.front: What does `service password-encryption` do, and what's its limitation?
cards.back: Encrypts plaintext passwords currently in running-config and any future ones using weak Type-7 (Vigenère cipher) — easily reversible with online tools. If you later issue `no service password-encryption`, already-encrypted passwords stay encrypted (it doesn't decrypt them); only newly-set passwords would be plaintext.

cards.front: Three Cisco commands that save running-config to startup-config?
cards.back: `copy running-config startup-config` (canonical), `write memory` (or just `write`), and the legacy `wr`. All do the same thing — overwrite NVRAM startup-config with the current RAM running-config.

cards.front: What does the `no` keyword do in Cisco IOS configuration?
cards.back: Negates / removes a previously configured command. Example: `no service password-encryption` disables encryption going forward. `no shutdown` brings up an interface. `no ip address` removes an IP. Most configuration commands have a `no` form that undoes them.
